source: FII Institute — Rise of the Gigafirm: next trillion-dollar tech company (panel)
summary: AI may ultimately be mediated through brain-computer interfaces, leading to human-AI co-evolution
summary_extended: Baker speculates that the long-term trajectory of AI may not culminate in external humanoid robots but rather in brain-computer interfaces (BCIs) that mediate human-AI interaction directly, enabling a form of human-AI co-evolution. He frames humanoid robots as merely an intermediate step on this longer arc, suggesting the endpoint is a more intimate integration of AI with human cognition. This is presented as a highly speculative, long-horizon vision rather than a near-term investable thesis, with Baker using explicitly hedged language ('may eventually'). No specific companies, timeframes, or financial metrics are cited, making this a philosophical framing of AI's trajectory rather than an actionable investment call.
detail: Baker speculates that the long-term future involves AI mediated through BCIs, with humans co-evolving with AI rather than AI being purely embodied in external robots. He frames humanoid robots as a first step toward this future.
confidence_evidence: Baker uses speculative language ('may eventually') and frames this as a long-term vision rather than near-term investment thesis
quote_fragment: AI may eventually be mediated through brain computer interfaces
